paper_title: CoSBC: Dependence-Specialized Enriched SBC with Symmetric Pooled Ranking
email_title: CoSBC: Dependence-Specialized Enriched SBC with Symmetric Pooled Ranking

sections.summary: This paper proposes CoSBC, a dependence-focused instantiation of enriched Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC) that replaces scalar ranking of dependence summaries with permutation-equivariant pooled multivariate scoring based on pooled marginal ranks. The method uses two pre-registered feature families (pairwise covariance and joint-tail co-occurrence) and calibrates family and global statistics via shared Monte Carlo relabelings of the planted index. Empirically, CoSBC yields modest power gains over a carefully feature-matched enriched-SBC baseline in a few Gaussian synthetic settings, but it exhibits notable null-calibration inflation and shows no advantage on a fixed-data posterior-SBC “radon” study; localization performance is also mixed.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The paper isolates and cleanly studies a single design change within enriched SBC: symmetric, permutation-equivariant pooled multivariate ranking of candidate features versus separate scalar ranking followed by aggregation.
The construction emphasizes exchangeability-preserving operations (pooled marginal ranks, leave-one-out distance scores), making the per-family rank validity argument transparent.
The attention to dependence via two targeted feature families (covariance and joint-tail) addresses a well-known limitation of coordinate-wise SBC.


Experimental rigor and validation
The experimental setup is controlled: CoSBC and the “matched enriched-SBC” comparator share the same transforms, feature banks, and calibration machinery, enabling a focused attribution of effects to pooled multivariate versus scalar ranking.
Multiple contextual baselines (scalar SBC, discriminative calibration, energy distance) provide reference points for sensitivity beyond SBC-style ranks.
Provenance and reproducibility are unusually strong; the manuscript links table rows to concrete CSV artifacts with hashes, and includes a small exhaustive-check sanity test for the permutation calibration.


Clarity of presentation
The paper is explicit about its scope: it does not propose a new SBC principle, but evaluates a specific pooled-ranking choice in an enriched-SBC pipeline.
Limitations are candidly discussed, including the central unresolved null-calibration issue and negative results on fixed data.
The symmetry claim and the construction are described in a way that makes auditing straightforward.


Significance of contributions
Even as a negative or mixed-result paper, it offers a careful, reproducible enriched-SBC instantiation focused on dependence, clarifying what pooled ranking does and does not add beyond feature-matched enriched SBC.
The work highlights practical failure modes (tail-family inflation, limited localization) that will be valuable for future SBC method design.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
The end-to-end null-calibration inflation (mean global rejection 0.1333 at α = 0.05) is substantial and unexplained; this undermines the central attractiveness of SBC’s exact finite-sample null.
The tail co-occurrence family uses hard thresholds (0.1/0.9) with small M (24), which can induce high variance and discreteness artifacts; this may be a structural source of the observed null skew.
The pooled multivariate score is a simple L2-average of leave-one-out distances; while permutation-equivariant, it may dilute feature-specific signals relative to scalar-ranking aggregation and offers no theoretical advantage beyond symmetry.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
The benchmark scope is narrow: conjugate Gaussian synthetic models and one fixed-data hierarchical regression. There is no evaluation on non-Gaussian, nonconjugate, or higher-dimensional posteriors common in modern Bayesian workflows.
The study uses only three random seeds and modest SBC budgets (R = 100, M = 24, B = 199). Sensitivity of null calibration and power to B, M, and R is only lightly explored despite the central calibration issue.
The localization assessment shows CoSBC underperforming enriched SBC on FlipPair; alternative localization-compatible scoring rules are not thoroughly explored.


Clarity or presentation issues
While the per-family rank validity is stated, the paper does not fully formalize conditions under which the global T_max calibration remains valid (e.g., conditioning, reuse of randomness, dependence across families).
Some figures and tables refer to post-hoc corrections and revised rows without a consolidated errata-style summary; this is mitigated by the artifacts but still slightly interrupts the flow.


Missing related work or comparisons
The paper does not engage with VSBC (Yao et al., 2018) as a complementary simulation-based diagnostic for approximate inference, which is germane when assessing variational and Laplace approximations.
Broader SBI diagnostics/benchmarks (e.g., Lueckmann et al., 2021 benchmark for likelihood-free inference) are not discussed, limiting context for multivariate calibration strategies.
Alternative dependence diagnostics (e.g., HSIC, distance covariance, copula-based tail dependence measures) are only lightly connected via energy/MMD ablations; stronger theoretical/empirical comparisons could be informative.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The per-family randomized-rank uniformity under exchangeability and permutation-equivariant scoring is sound and aligns with SBC’s core logic. The implementation acknowledges tie-breaking and uses pooled marginal rank transforms that preserve exchangeability.
The unresolved issue is the global calibration via Monte Carlo relabelings with T_max across families. In principle, a permutation test that redraws the planted index while recomputing all downstream statistics (including any randomness) should deliver exact finite-sample control. The observed inflation—concentrated in the tail family—suggests either: (i) a violation of permutation-invariance in some subtle component (e.g., thresholding behavior under small M, randomness reuse), (ii) insufficient B and discretization effects for the chosen statistic S_l, or (iii) the combining rule (max over families) interacting with non-exchangeable nuisance randomness. A deeper theoretical and empirical audit is necessary.
The choice of tail indicators at fixed 0.1/0.9 post-PIT thresholds may produce sparse, high-variance features when M is small; this can compromise the stability of S_l and induce p-value skew even under nominal per-family uniform ranks.


Experimental evaluation assessment
Synthetic power: Gains over feature-matched enriched SBC occur in only three settings (0.3333 absolute rejection). These are directionally interesting but limited; energy distance sometimes dominates.
Null studies: The inflation is not a minor fluctuation; with α = 0.05, 0.1333 rejection on average is problematic. The attribution to the tail family is plausible based on p-value distributions, but not conclusive.
Localization: CoSBC’s pooled scoring appears to dilute feature-level signals, leading to worse top-1 recovery than enriched SBC on FlipPair; this undermines one of the intended practical benefits.
Fixed-data posterior SBC: Despite substantial covariance errors across approximations, both CoSBC and enriched SBC fail to reject. This indicates that after pooled marginal rank transform, the chosen dependence features are not sensitive to real posterior discrepancies in this setting. The severity correlations are modest and do not translate to detection.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
Relative to Talts et al. (2018), this work preserves the SBC null logic but attempts a more multivariate dependence-sensitive ranking. It aligns methodologically but does not extend the theory.
Versus enriched SBC (Modrák et al., 2022), the study cleanly isolates the effect of pooled multivariate scoring; the gains are modest and context-dependent, suggesting that better features may matter more than pooling per se.
Discriminative calibration (Yao & Domke, 2023) is an informative comparator and often sensitive, though it lacks exact rank semantics; this paper correctly treats it as context rather than a drop-in replacement.
VSBC (Yao et al., 2018) is absent from the discussion. While VSBC and PSIS address different questions (average marginal calibration and joint proposal quality), they are important baselines for approximate inference diagnostics and could contextualize the fixed-data results.
Broader SBI benchmarks (e.g., 2101.04653) and dependence measures (HSIC, distance covariance) could help triage which multivariate features retain power after PIT pooling and in small-M regimes.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The paper offers a careful, negative-leaning evaluation that helps the community understand the limited added value of pooled multivariate ranking when enriched SBC already uses dependence features. This clarity is valuable.
However, the unresolved null-calibration problem directly challenges SBC’s core promise; publishing without at least diagnosing or bounding the issue risks confusion for practitioners.
The strong reproducibility culture is commendable and sets a high standard for empirical SBC work.
sections.questions_for_authors:
Can you precisely specify the permutation calibration protocol: for each relabeling, do you recompute the entire pipeline (including pooled ranks, feature computation, LOO scores, and all random tie-jitters) with shared random seeds, or are some intermediate quantities held fixed? A step-by-step algorithmic listing would help diagnose the tail-family inflation.
Have you examined larger B (e.g., B ≥ 2000) and different combining functions (Fisher, Tippett, NPC/Westfall-Young stepdown) to see whether null inflation persists? If so, please report those results and whether the inflation remains concentrated in the tail family.
How sensitive are the tail-family p-values to the U-thresholds (0.1/0.9) and to M? Would using smoothed tail scores (e.g., soft indicators, empirical tail-copula estimators, or rank-based co-exceedance counts with adaptive quantiles ensuring balanced counts) mitigate the observed skew?
In the fixed-data posterior-SBC setting, can you confirm that the pooled transform and feature computation are fully within-replicate exchangeability-preserving? For example, is any tuning (e.g., standardization) performed using information that could inadvertently break permutation invariance?
For localization, have you tried hybrid schemes that retain per-pair scalar ranking (for attribution) while using pooled multivariate scoring only for detection, or multiscale features (pairs, triplets) to avoid dilution? Any preliminary results?
Could you add non-Gaussian fixed-data models (e.g., logistic or Poisson GLMMs, or tasks from SBI benchmarks) to probe whether the null and power behaviors generalize beyond the current radon setup?
Can you provide a small theoretical analysis for S_l under uniform PITs (e.g., its exact/permutation null distribution for finite R, M) to clarify when the Monte Carlo calibration should be exact and what failure modes are plausible?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    4.5/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This is a careful and transparent empirical study of a narrow but relevant design choice in enriched SBC: replacing scalar ranking with symmetric pooled multivariate ranking on dependence features. The paper’s strengths are its methodological clarity, strong reproducibility, and honest reporting of mixed and negative results. However, the central finding—a persistent and unexplained null-calibration inflation—undercuts SBC’s primary appeal, and the practical benefits are modest: limited synthetic gains, no improvement on a realistic fixed-data task, and weaker localization. The experimental scope is also relatively narrow. While I appreciate the contribution as a well-executed negative result and an auditable baseline for future work, the current version falls short of ICLR’s bar in terms of resolved methodology and demonstrated impact. I encourage the authors to first diagnose and remedy the null-calibration issue, broaden the benchmarks (including non-Gaussian and higher-dimensional cases), and strengthen the localization design before publication at a top-tier venue.

                                Summary
                            
                            This paper proposes CoSBC, a dependence-focused instantiation of enriched Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC) that replaces scalar ranking of dependence summaries with permutation-equivariant pooled multivariate scoring based on pooled marginal ranks. The method uses two pre-registered feature families (pairwise covariance and joint-tail co-occurrence) and calibrates family and global statistics via shared Monte Carlo relabelings of the planted index. Empirically, CoSBC yields modest power gains over a carefully feature-matched enriched-SBC baseline in a few Gaussian synthetic settings, but it exhibits notable null-calibration inflation and shows no advantage on a fixed-data posterior-SBC “radon” study; localization performance is also mixed.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Strengths
                            
                            
Technical novelty and innovation
The paper isolates and cleanly studies a single design change within enriched SBC: symmetric, permutation-equivariant pooled multivariate ranking of candidate features versus separate scalar ranking followed by aggregation.
The construction emphasizes exchangeability-preserving operations (pooled marginal ranks, leave-one-out distance scores), making the per-family rank validity argument transparent.
The attention to dependence via two targeted feature families (covariance and joint-tail) addresses a well-known limitation of coordinate-wise SBC.


Experimental rigor and validation
The experimental setup is controlled: CoSBC and the “matched enriched-SBC” comparator share the same transforms, feature banks, and calibration machinery, enabling a focused attribution of effects to pooled multivariate versus scalar ranking.
Multiple contextual baselines (scalar SBC, discriminative calibration, energy distance) provide reference points for sensitivity beyond SBC-style ranks.
Provenance and reproducibility are unusually strong; the manuscript links table rows to concrete CSV artifacts with hashes, and includes a small exhaustive-check sanity test for the permutation calibration.


Clarity of presentation
The paper is explicit about its scope: it does not propose a new SBC principle, but evaluates a specific pooled-ranking choice in an enriched-SBC pipeline.
Limitations are candidly discussed, including the central unresolved null-calibration issue and negative results on fixed data.
The symmetry claim and the construction are described in a way that makes auditing straightforward.
